title: `podman build` manual's `cpp` suggestion is incomplete
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

/kind bug

**Description**

The `podman-build` man page mentions using `cpp -E` to preprocess `Containerfile.in` files, but fails to mention that doing so will fail if output is not directed to stdout and there are any comments, or that all comments will be removed. This means it's not possible to have a readable `Dockerfile` generated from a `Containerfile.in`.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.
```sh
% cat <<. | cpp -E - -o out
FROM scratch
# add a nice label
LABEL foo bar
.
<stdin>:2:3: error: invalid preprocessing directive #add
```

2.
```sh
% test -f out; echo $?
1
```

**Describe the results you received:**

No file produced

**Describe the results you expected:**

File produced, unchanged except for `#include` statements being replaced.

comments.body:
The question would be how could we tell the difference.  I guess we could remove comments that did not include known C directives.
```
# include
# ifdef
# if
# endef
```
Others ?

I'd suggest removing the mention of `cpp` from the manual entirely, as attempting to use a `Containerfile.in` as a template for both podman and docker is just hiding rakes for someone to step on later. Any file processed by either `cpp` or podman will remove the load-bearing comments (parser directives).

I can't tell by reading code if podman handles the `escape` parser directive correctly, but if it does, using `cpp` as a templating mechanism would silently change the meaning.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
